content.text: The test is failing because the mock for the `/users/me` endpoint isn't being applied—the server is returning the actual user data instead of the mocked 500 error. I need to check how the mock is configured to see why it's not intercepting that request.

I'm checking the test mocks to understand why the `/users/me` endpoint is returning a 500 error in the logs when the bootstrap data shows user and tenant information being returned successfully—there's a mismatch between what the mock is configured to do and what's actually happening.

I'm noticing a discrepancy—the other tests pass with their mock bodies, but the root-error test with a 500 status and "Internal Server Error" message isn't being applied, even though the server logs show the bootstrap data loaded successfully. Let me trace through how the request mocking is actually being handled.

I'm checking the RMP library documentation to understand how status handling works with string bodies and content-type expectations. Looking at the test output, I notice the bootstrap data is fully logged even though only the `/users/me` mock was configured, which suggests the mock either didn't match properly or the setup order is causing issues with the regex matching.

Let me trace through the URL construction in `requestOrgApiJson` - for "/users/me" it should build `${apiHostname}/users/me` and the regex pattern should match that. But I'm realizing the real issue: if the `/users/me` mock actually matched, it would succeed and then trigger `fetchTenants`, which has no mock set up and should throw an error. Since the logs show complete bootstrap data including tenants, it means the mock never matched at all and real API calls are going through - which suggests this might be running against a live backend in E2E mode rather than using mocked responses.

content.text: The backend is actually running locally and returning real data, which means the mock isn't intercepting the request properly. I'm checking the RMP library documentation to see if there's a different wrapper format needed—maybe the response needs to be structured differently than what I'm currently using.
The schema validation looks good with the 500 status and string body, so now I need to trace through the client code to figure out where things are breaking down.

The URL regex matching seems fine, but I'm noticing an issue with how the method gets handled. When we call `mockServerRequest.GET(urls.usersMe, ...)` in the test, it sets the method to "GET", but the request-mocking-protocol client uses `addMockWithMethod` to configure this. The problem is that when we only pass a URL regex and response schema without explicitly specifying the method, the matcher defaults to checking for "GET", which happens to match what the fetch call in `org-api.server.ts` is using.

Let me trace through what's actually happening here. The bootstrap console log is showing real user data, which means the mock isn't being applied at all—the actual backend is being hit. The other tests in `smoke.spec.ts` work fine, but they mock both `/users/me` and `/tenants`, whereas our failing test only mocks `/users/me`. I need to look at the fetch interceptor to understand why the mock isn't being applied when only one endpoint is mocked.

I'm re-examining the test output more carefully to see if the error is actually being thrown but maybe the RootError component is rendering with a different testid or structure than expected.

Looking at the logged data, the `_authed` loader's console output shows complete appBootstrap data, which means `getAppBootstrapData()` actually succeeded — but the test mocked `/users/me` to return a 500 error. That's strange. Let me trace through the test sequence: it sets up the mock with the status 500 response first, then navigates to the page. So either the mock isn't being applied correctly, or something about how the environment variables or request headers are configured is preventing the mock from intercepting the request. I'm wondering if parallel test execution with `workers: 5` could be causing mock conflicts, but each test gets its own Playwright context so headers shouldn't clash. Let me verify the mock setup is actually working by adding debug logging, and I should also check if the `onChange` callback is being awaited properly before `page.goto` proceeds.

content.text:
The mock is working and throwing the `OrgApiError` with a 500 status, but the root error boundary isn't catching it. I'm realizing the error bubbles up to the `_authed` loader which throws, and since that route doesn't have an `errorComponent` defined, TanStack Router isn't rendering the error UI as expected.

Let me check what the actual error page looks like by examining the error-context.md file to see what's being rendered in the DOM.

The page is displaying TanStack Router's default error boundary UI instead of our custom `RootError` component, which suggests the error is being caught at the `_authed` route level—but since that route doesn't have its own `errorComponent` defined, I'd expect it to bubble up to the parent. Let me think through what's actually happening here.

Looking at how TanStack Router handles errors: if a route doesn't specify an `errorComponent`, it falls back to the default global error UI rather than delegating to the parent. Since the loader error occurs in `_authed` and that route has no error component, we're seeing the default "Something went wrong!" message.

The test expects the root error boundary to render for any server error in the app, so the cleanest solution is to set a `defaultErrorComponent` at the router level rather than adding error components to individual routes.

I found a `defaultErrorComponent` option on the router that I can use. Let me set `RootError` as the default error component by updating the router configuration. Since `RootError` already accepts the right props type and uses hooks that should work in this context, I'll go ahead and make that change.
Now let me run the test to verify:

input.command: pnpm exec playwright test tests/root-error.spec.ts --reporter=line 2>&1 | tail -30

content.text: Test passes. Let me also run the full test suite to make sure I haven't broken anything:

input.new_string:
## Error boundaries

Any route loader/component error that isn't caught by a route-specific
`errorComponent` falls through to `defaultErrorComponent` on the router
(`src/router.tsx`), which is wired to `RootError`
(`src/components/root-error.tsx`). The root route also sets
`errorComponent: RootError` so the same UI renders for errors thrown from
the root loader (e.g. the user-session loader).

- Covered automatically by `tests/root-error.spec.ts` (mocks `/users/me`
  as 500 and asserts `data-testid="root-error"`).
- `RootError` treats `OrgApiError` with `status === 401` as a session
  expiry message. 401s from server functions are otherwise redirected to
  `/login` by `authMiddleware` before they can reach the boundary.

## Request mocking for tests

content.text:
Summary of changes:

- `apps/org-next/src/router.tsx`: imported `RootError` and set `defaultErrorComponent: RootError` on `createRouter`, so route errors (like the `_authed` loader's `OrgApiError`) render our `RootError` (which carries `data-testid="root-error"`) instead of TanStack Router's built-in "Something went wrong!" fallback.
- `apps/org-next/docs/README.md`: added a short "Error boundaries" section documenting the wiring and the covering test, per the `docs/` maintenance rule in `AGENTS.md`.

Full test suite: 8 passed.
